title: When One Says Tiruchi, Is It Not a Turning-Point Conference? (2)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source is authoritative for this numbered title series, historical quotations, names and the affectionate spoken form `Thanna` near the close. OCR repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy.
paragraphs: You will have read what I began writing last Sunday about when the DMK's State Conferences were held, where they took place and who presided over them, as well as the tenth State Conference to be held in Tiruchi. I also explained the historic democratic decision at the second Tiruchi State Conference, where ballot boxes were placed before the delegates to determine whether the DMK should contest elections, and the significance of holding the tenth conference in the same Tiruchi before the coming parliamentary general election.

I had already mentioned that the DMK, born in 1949, conducted its first State Conference in Chennai on 13, 14, 15 and 16 December 1951 under Perarignar Anna's leadership. Before that, on the morning of 17 November 1951, the General Council met in Madurai. Today, do not the executive and general councils of some parties begin at three in the afternoon and finish by six? The DMK General Council that assembled that morning concluded only the following morning. With no interval, many were given an opportunity to express their views and the debates continued throughout.

Kudanthai K.K. Neelamegam proposed Comrade E.V.K. Sampath to inaugurate the first State Conference scheduled for Chennai that December. I proposed Navalar Nedunchezhian. In the meantime, Anna announced the decision that K.K. Neelamegam himself should inaugurate it. Perarignar Anna as conference president, the elder K.K. Neelamegam as inaugurator, and elder brother C.P. Chitrarasu as inaugurator of the exhibition were elected unanimously.

How many memories surround that first State Conference! Work on it proceeded day and night in Chennai. Anna and all of us remained in the conference pavilion around the clock, attending to the arrangements. One night at eleven, Anna, I and several friends were sitting and talking atop a mound of sand deposited for spreading across the pavilion. At the Kumari district conference held in Nagercoil before the State Conference, local friends had prevented Kalaivanar N.S. Krishnan from attending, leaving him deeply hurt. I told Anna and asked that Kalaivanar be invited to the State Conference. Anna immediately said, “Go now and bring him.”

There were no mobile phones then as there are now. I took the car and went directly to Kalaivanar's home. The moment I said Anna had invited him, tears poured from Kalaivanar's eyes like a waterfall, and he climbed into the car. Can one ever forget the sight of Kalaivanar reaching the sand mound beside the pavilion and embracing Anna, or Anna welcoming him in a voice choked with emotion? “What is this, Anna? Am I not part of this conference?” Kalaivanar asked. Anna replied, “Why, the conference itself begins with your villuppattu, does it not?”

In the 1951 Madurai General Council, I proposed the inaugurator of the first State Conference. For the tenth State Conference to be held in Tiruchi this February, it has now been announced on behalf of the party that I shall preside and hoist the flag; our General Secretary, Inamana Perasiriyar, will inaugurate it; party treasurer Thalapathi Thambi M.K. Stalin will serve as conference treasurer; and Tiruchi district secretary Thambi K.N. Nehru will chair the reception committee.

Because the name of Anbil, the Dravidian warrior given to us by Tiruchi and unforgettable to both the party and me, has already been used and announced many times, the pavilion for the tenth State Conference has been named after my dear Thambi Veerapandi S. Arumugam, the “Lion of Salem” who developed the party in the district adjoining Tiruchi. His sons Raja and Prabhu, district party officer Sivalingam, Panamarathupatti Rajendran and party Udanpirappugal met me and expressed their gratitude.

The stage has been named after Sister Vasuki Murugesan, a valiant woman who became Karur district secretary at a young age, demonstrated that women could efficiently discharge that responsibility, performed splendid work, and died in a road accident. District officer Nanniyur Rajendran, Kulithalai Sivaraman, Vasuki's husband Murugesan, Thambi Ravikumar and friends met me to express their thanks.

Likewise, the hall bears the name of Porselvi Ilamurugu, the distinguished elderly woman given by Kulithalai who began movement work in youth and continued without slackening even into extreme old age. The front facade is named for M.S. Venkatachalam, who developed an exceptional ability to speak and write in both Tamil and English, earned Anna's affection, worked beside him and has since passed away. The inner facade is named for Ma. Balakrishnan, who served with distinction as Tiruchi district treasurer and Mayor. The entrance is named for Poondi K. Kalaichelvan, the DMK stalwart who served as Tiruvarur district secretary and was murdered by cruel men. The flag platform is named for Thambi K.N. Ramajayam, brother of district secretary Thambi Nehru and a brave man whom I had expected to labour for many years to develop the party in Tiruchi. Conference work is proceeding systematically under these names. Ma. Balakrishnan's son Baranikumar, his family and friends, and Tiruvarur district secretary Kalaivanan also expressed their gratitude.

After this tenth State Conference, we shall face the Indian parliamentary general election. At the first State Conference it was decided that the party would participate in general elections. Later, after prolonged discussion at the Madurai General Council, it was decided that the DMK would not field its own candidates in the 1952 election, but would support Communists and only those who applied to party headquarters for DMK support, signed the agreement prescribed by the party and obtained the General Secretary's approval.

As the first event of the first State Conference in Chennai on 13 December 1951, a massive procession set out from the party's Arivagam and reached the pavilion. General Secretary and conference president Anna hoisted the party's two-coloured flag. The next day, after K.K. Neelamegam inaugurated the conference and spoke, N.V. Natarajan proposed that Anna preside over it. The list of seconders included Navalar, E.V.K. Sampath, C.P. Chitrarasu, Dr Dharmambal, K.A. Mathiazhagan, Thillai Villalan, Parangusam, Sathyavani Muthu, Anbil Dharmalingam, M.S. Sivasamy, Palanisamy, S.A. Rasamanickam, Nadippisai Pulavar K.R. Ramasamy, Mullai Vadivelu, Nanjilar and C.V.M. Annamalai, as well as me.

Seconding Anna's leadership, I said then: “Life is three letters; the character life needs is three letters; the affection born of character is three letters; the love that wells from affection is three letters; the valour produced by love is three letters; the field to which the brave go is three letters; the victory won in that field is three letters; and ‘Anna,’ who leads us along the path to that victory, is three letters. I second that Anna for the leadership.”

I have not forgotten what I said 62 years ago when seconding my Anna, and no one who knows me has forgotten it. Yet that Anna of mine departed 43 years ago, silently issuing the command that I should lead this party. No, no. He has not disappeared, and we do not consider him gone. He remains mingled in our words, our deeds and the pulse of our lives!

It was at that first conference that Anna taught us this lesson: “We walk peacefully towards our ideal. Problems encountered on that journey are like friends met along the road. We shall talk as we travel and then part, but we shall not abandon the path. If it rains, we shall take shelter for a little while, but we shall not cease. We shall continue walking. Mist, rain, sun, cold, night or day will neither halt the journey nor alter the path. Corpses may lie along it. There may be skeletons. Vultures may circle them and jackals may howl. But we shall not lose our way. We shall walk on, treating the corpses and all else as signs that warriors have gone before us. We shall not lose the fundamental ideal for which we were created and for which we labour merely because problems arise along the way.”

At the first State Conference in 1951, I seconded Perarignar Anna. At the second State Conference, held in this same city of Tiruchi on 17, 18, 19 and 20 May 1956, Anna himself seconded Navalar Nedunchezhian as president. Who can forget those historic words? “Thambi, come! Come and assume the leadership! We shall obey your command! Come!” Those were Anna's diamond lines in proposing Navalar.

In his concluding address, Anna later explained those words: “Though he may be my younger brother, once we have all chosen someone as General Secretary, obeying his command and respecting that leadership is what develops party discipline. I have followed this practice of respecting leadership for many years in my political life.” Thus Anna explained “discipline.”

How many conferences, General Councils and Executive Committees did Perarignar Anna attend, teaching us discipline and guiding us on our journey towards the ideal? Today he is not here, but the party he created, our very breath of life, remains. It flourishes like a banyan and rises towards the sky. Its tenth State Conference will be held splendidly in Tiruchi.

Today's *Dinamani*, dated 4 January 2014, carries a prominent report with a photograph of pavilion construction. Reception committee chairman K.N. Nehru says that work is underway on approximately 250 acres near Dheeran Nagar on Dindigul Road; the pavilion will stretch for one kilometre; it will have a sheet-metal roof so that rain cannot interrupt the conference; pavilion construction will finish by the end of this month; work has begun on borewells at 20 places; and wall-advertising work has commenced.

Among Udanpirappugal like you, who have merged inseparably with the party and laboured throughout your lives, only a few will have attended all ten State Conferences. Only a few who attended the first and second will remain. Can you miss the tenth?

Reception committee chairman Thambi K.N. Nehru travelled here by car to convey New Year greetings. After greeting me at about 8.30 p.m., he left that very night by car for Tiruchi. When I asked, “Travel at this hour?” he pleaded affectionately, “I have work in the morning, Thanna,” and went. He left, but I worried that he must reach home safely and return. Is it not the affection I bear for Udanpirappugal that makes me worry about the party, the party, the party even at this age?

Let my concern be. January has begun. Pongal is next week, followed in the first week of February by our Anna's memorial day. The days will pass just like that. Set out on your journey. Come safely. My eyes will be waiting for your arrival!
Affectionately,
M.K.
5 January 2014
